sentences.text: My name is Alex Reifer, A-L-E-X.
Reifer is R-E-I-- F like Frank-- E-R.
I was given the name I-S-E-R, Iser Reifer, R-E-I-F-E-R.
I-- now I have a middle name, because they call me Alex I Reifer.
From the 'Iser.'
I was born February 7th, 1921.
City was Oswiecim.
Today, the Germans after the when the war start, they calling it Auschwitz.
The country is Poland.
Only Iser.
[NODS]
My grand-- grandfather was a rabbi, but he didn't perform the title of rabbi for the city.
He-- he was a businessman.
He owned one of the biggest houses in Oswiecim-- 46 apartments.
And when he was younger, he was the biggest in Poland in egg.
He was-- he was buying and selling eggs to wholesalers.
He was an egg professional.
And after that, he settled down.
He bought, uh, in Oswiecim, this house, this building, and he remodeled it.
But with the modeling in the house-- which, the whole house was not air-conditioned.
It was not heating.
And the bathrooms were outside, not in the apartments.
And it was no gas.
It was only rooms.
And in the kitchen was-- the kitchens was only you can use is coal, or wood, or something.
It was no running water.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this was the way I was born in this particular house.
My grandfather's name was Meyer Reifer.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was my father's father.
My father was the second from the children which was born.
And all of them was in good shape, I mean, in good condition, but in this particular city.
My father's name was [?
Berek ?] Reifer.
In Jewish, it's Berl Reifer.
My mother's name was Miriam Reifer.
And, uh, my father was born also in Oswiecim.
And my mother was born in Tarnow-- also Poland.
And the reason they meet is through a shadchan-- we're calling it a shadchan.
It means a person which put together two people.
And, uh, they didn't meet before the wedding.
This is the way the very religious people do.
And it was make, they got the wedding.
And I was born in 1921.
The wedding was in 1919.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And, uh, we have always got maid in the house.
Because in-- in this particular part, you need to have a maid.
If you don't have a maid, you must bring your own water every two days in the house.
We have got in the corner, special bowl, special maid.
And the maid bring, or we have got a special woman which bring in twice a week, three times a week, water from-- from the-- from the main station, from the water station.
And the water station was around half a mile, a little bit more than half a mile, from our house.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is the way I was born.
And in our congregation, and the-- and the religious Jewish people, you start school when we was three years old.
And, uh, I started before, because my father teach-- excuse me.
[DRINKS] In three years old, I was in the Jewish school.
And my father paid a special person which come to the house, pick me up, bring me to school, pick me up from school, bring me home everyday.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And school was when I was young, three years old, until five.
We was going to school.
They pick us up 8:00 in the morning.
And my mother give me lunch-- a piece of bread with not butter, but it was very expensive, margarine.
And an apple.
This was my lunch.
And when I came home, we eat dinner.
And this is the way it was, my children's life.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My second brother's name was Leopold.
He was born 1923.
And my third brother name was [?
Emanuel, ?] and he was born two years later, 1925.
My fourth brother was Chaim.
He was born 1927.
And my fifth brother was born 1929, I think, or 1920-- no, 1931.
His name was Hersh Joseph.
He got his name from my mother's father, and mother father was a rabbi.
And he passed away in this particular year when my youngest brother was born, and he got the name.
This is the way we give the names, from people, from family, to remember what's going on in the past.
Their birthday?
Oh, the bris!
Oh, yeah.
A bris?
Oh, we have got the party in the house.
We bring a mohel-- mohel, they calling it mohel, and the person which is a professional.
And you have a special cutting knife.
And what he doing is, uh-- they holding the penis and they cut it off, a certain part of the skin only.
They don't cut off the penis.
A lot of people don't understand maybe what is going on in the Jewish religion.
The skin-- they took off-- they picked up the skin from the penis, and they cut off a piece from the skin.
The reason is, what you do that, and in 100 years before that, the people were sick from this particular item, because they cover up the penis, and they couldn't-- they are not breathing inside.
And maybe today, it is the same.
I don't know.
Maybe after 100 years, [INAUDIBLE] is different.
But this is the way it was.
Yeah.
Uh, normally, we-- what we was doing was we was going every Saturday to schul, and with all the family-- maybe it was old already, or young, whatever it was.
And we was-- and members from our schul, from also from our family, they're calling it the [?
Shankus Stevel. ?] And the name-- the name was [?
Shankus. ?] And he give-- the whole building, he give for these people, which did for the congregation.
He give it free, away.
He didn't charge anything for it.
And down there, we-- we was going Friday night.
And we was going Saturday morning to schul, to pray.
And we came back, and then we come home, so we talking about Friday night and Saturday.
But Friday night-- normally, it was like a holiday.
Every Friday night and Saturday was like a holiday.
The reason was we are not people which-- when I was young, which drink a lot of alcohol.
But my mother used to do, she prepared Thursday-- um, Thursday, the grapes-- the grapes from [INAUDIBLE].
And she put a little bit sugar, and she put it away in a warm side.
And this, Friday night, she drain it.
And we was drinking this for kiddush like wine.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is the way it was.
And then we-- we baked challah.
Every Thursday, my mother baked challah.
And, uh-- we have got-- by the dinner, we have got-- always, we got two kind of fish.
We got gefilte fish and carp.
After that, we got soup with all kinds of different items in it-- different items.
Sometimes beans, or sometimes something else in it.
And after that, we got the chicken.
Friday night, normally is-- is a preparation for Shabbat.
And, uh-- what we did-- Friday night, we still can have hot meals from our stove.
And, um-- and then after that, what we did every Friday, when I was a little bit older, the preparation which my mother made was for whole Saturdays.
And Saturdays, we-- we could not cook.
That what we did.
We bring the food which is prepared for Saturday during the day-- we-- I bring the food to a bakery.
And the bakery put it in the oven, in the side of the oven, to have it warm.
And I pick up for Saturday-- I was going to pick up the food hot.
And always, we have got hot food for Saturday.
And then that what we have got.
And we have got compote-- compote is dessert.
We have got different type of food, apples.
And this is the way we-- we passed through the holidays.
Also, uh, when I was a little bit older, I-- my grandfather has got something problem with his eyes.
My grandfather, Meyer Reifer, from the father's father.
And, uh, the-- the doctor send him to Czechoslovakia for operations.
And twice, he was-- operation, they kill both eyes.
And I was his eyes from this time.
And my grandfather was very religious.
And eveyday in the morning, he is go-- was going to the mikvah.
You know what the mikvah means.
A mikvah is a ritual-- ritual spa.
It's a special house, spa house, which you go to wash yourself, or you dip in.
And I took my grandfather after that, when he didn't see.
I was his eyes.
Eveyday in the morning, it was 6:00 in the morning, I wake up.
And I took him to the mikvah.
And from the mikvah, I took him to schul, everyday to schul to pray, and then I bring him home.
And, uh-- he-- I was the only person which did that all the time.
And when I left, and I was older, and I left, my brother took my place.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Everyday, in the morning.
I was very religious.
I got, uh, sight [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was very religious.
And, uh, until I-- I became a little bit less religious when I was older.
But in the time when I was in school until five years, and then they transfer me to a different rabbi when I was older.
When five years old, my father made a party.
He calling it the [HEBREW] seder.
[HEBREW] seder is-- [HEBREW] means, this is the fifth book from Moses is [HEBREW].
[HEBREW] seder.
He made a party, and they asked me, the rabbi asked me questions.
And I answered, but [INAUDIBLE] all the time.
After that, they transferred me to a higher rabbi when I was five, to a different rabbi.
And I learned more, a little bit more.
Like you go here to the school, the regular school, and you learn when you are 8, and 7, 9 years old.
We learned this when we was five in Jewish religion.
And when I-- when I was seven, they transfer me to a higher rabbi.
And I was very, very, very religious.
I was religious when I got seven years old, I was going to two schools.
When I was seven years old, I was going to the city school-- we calling it the Polish school.
And in the morning, I was going to the Polish school, 8 o'clock.
I came home 1 o'clock.
And I was going to the Jewish school, and I came home at 5 o'clock, and sometimes, 6:00.
And after that-- after that, I have got a special teacher, which I was going everyday for an hour, hour and a half.
Not too far, but-- but it was two-- two streets from our house.
And my father paid for it, and she teach me I should pick up more from the Polish school.
You know, because I got two schools, a little bit too much for a child like I am.
But my-- I got a good head, very good-- I used to have.
Now, I-- I don't know.
But I still have.
And, uh, we was doing all the time like that, until I was going-- until I finished the school until I was 13 years old.
And I was going two schools everyday.
Normally, Friday night, I meet my friends, and Saturday, I meet my friends.
But during the week, I have not got time even to play, because when I go from one school to the other one.
And after I come home, I eat something.
And I must go-- I must go to the preparation teacher which prepare for me for the next day all kinds of questions, and was-- was busy all the time.
And when I come home, even I also read a lot.
I was reading very good Polish, very good.
And then in the house, we speak several languages already.
In the house.
We speak Polish, Yiddish.
We speak German.
You see, we-- we speak a lot, you know.
And, uh, also, I speak a little bit of Russian in this particular time when I was young.
My mother teach me.
My mother speak that.
She is not from Russia, but she-- she was intelligent person.
And she has got friends, Russian friends when she was young, and she learned the Russian language.
And it's-- for me, it was good, because in the future, I-- I knew it.
And this is the way it was when I go from one rabbi to the other one.
And when I was 11 years old, they send me to the bobov yeshivot.
Bobov yeshivot means, like, here you go, and high school special, this is.
And the rabbi was a cousin of mine, Reifer.
He was the head of the yeshivot.
He was the head rabbi for the yeshivot.
And he prepared for me for the bar mitzvah for 13 years old.
And he teach me two years, from 11 to 13 years.
I was very religious down there.
And uh, I was going-- in the meantime, I was going several times to the bobov rabbi himself.
He was a very, very, very, very big person in Poland.
Very big person, the bobov rabbi.
He has got-- in every city, he has got a synagogue.
And, uh, he has got at least in Poland alone, he has got-- he has got, in Poland alone, over 300,000 followers.
He was big, very big.
And then [INAUDIBLE] was his-- it's a city in Poland, which was the headquarters.
Very big headquarters.
And I was prepared-- I was going normally every month, every six weeks, every eight weeks, I was going to the bobov rabbi.
He knew me already.
I mean, it's not easy to get to the rabbi, but I got in.
He knew me, and I-- I was a friend of his son.
He's a big rabbi now, also-- yeah, now.
And he knows me.
In-- in New York.
Yeah.
Um, [?
Hershel-- ?] [?
Hershel. ?] I don't know his second name, but bobov rabbi is, um-- I have it written down.
I forgot it already, his name.
We-- we never call a rabbi by-- a big rabbi like that with the name.
Nobody calling that.
And, uh, I was-- I was prepared for this [HEBREW].
[HEBREW] means speech from the Talmud.
And from the higher Talmud, we calling it [HEBREW], then they calling [HEBREW], and then we calling [HEBREW], and they calling [HEBREW].
This is, uh-- it's not easy to learn that.
Very tough.
Took me two years.
And I was prepared to go to the bobov rabbi [?
Shabeen. ?] And I've got a dinner.
It was over 400 people on the dinner.
And I was standing, uh, on the top of the table, and I hold the speech.
And a lot of people ask me different questions from the things, and I told them everything.
I-- I never fail.
And I did tremendous.
This is-- was my bar-- later, we make a bar mitzvah at the house.
But this was my mitzvah, and this was a prestige, and that I did it by the bobov rabbi.
Yeah.
The-- the bobov rabbi, after the-- the speech, he came to me, he give me a kiss.
[SOBS]
[DRINKS]
I remember, but I don't want to--
Now, I don't know, remember.
No, I don't want to--
Oh, yeah.
Sure, I got-- [HEBREW] when I was 13 years old, [HEBREW].
Sure, [? talis. ?] I got a small [? talis, ?] sure.
Hasidic, bobov Hasid, we calling it.
I started, but the Kabbalah is a very tough learning.
But I-- I learned all old type of [HEBREW], the toughest [HEBREW] I learned, the toughest one.
I have here.
I have-- some parts I hold.
Some parts I hold.
I respect people, just the Jewish religion teach you.
You know, I give always the woman the first things to go, just the way they bring you up.
The Talmud is very, very important, very interested.
The writers in the Talmud, not the five books of Moses, but the writers from the [INAUDIBLE] Talmud was geniuses, which do that.
No, no, no.
I love to study, yeah.
Yeah, I still study today, any kind, you know.
It was a synagogue, and they call me to the Torah.
And, uh-- I hold a small speech, also, in the house.
Not in the house, but in Oswiecim, and I hold a small speech.
And then my father invite people to the house, and we make a party.
And this is the way it was.
And from this time up, I, everyday, I lay the [HEBREW].
Everyday in the morning.
No, I don't do it.
I'm not religious now.
But I-- I have respect for a lot of people.
For the religious people, I have respect.
I, you know, the-- every person which has thinking for the past, whatever it was, for the past, I respect them.
Yeah.
I played sometimes.
I played-- we got a group later on, people-- football, soccer.
We calling it football, but it's soccer.
In-- in Poland, we call football soccer.
This I played when I was older, see?
But then-- also, with the [INAUDIBLE], but I made it smaller.
And, uh, I still have friends which I grow up with-- I showed you the pictures already.
One is-- one is living in Germany, [INAUDIBLE].
His name is [?
Schlomig ?] Beter-- Salomon Beter is his name.
And, uh, I have other friend-- there's a guy, David.
He lives in Paris.
He is a multi-multi-millionnaire.
He was also-- after, later on, he was a partner of mine.
This will become later on.
I will explain you about it.
No.
No, my father-- my father's got a grocery chain, a grocery store, big one.
And my mother helped him in the store.
And when I have time-- hour, I have hour, I also run in and help.
I like to sell.
When I was a kid, same thing.
Reifer Store, that's all.
We didn't make any corporations.
Nowadays, there are different type.
He has different-- in this particular age, in this particular year, it's different as it was 50, 60 years ago.
All of them.
Jewish and non-Jewish, to everybody which comes, he sold.
Oh, yeah, very Jewish.
We close-- we close the store Friday.
Late Friday, we close before we go to schul.
We was going to schul every Friday, Saturday in the morning.
And, uh, we was closed all Saturday.
We didn't open Saturday.
Only, we opened Sunday.
